title: Hand-drawn zine cover design, layout and lettering for the half-fold format
subtitle: How to plan, draw, and prepare a cover for an eight-page A5 zine
excerpt: A well-designed zine cover communicates the contents at a glance, works at A5 scale, and survives photocopying without losing legibility. This article covers cover layout for the standard half-fold zine format: title placement, image zones, white space, and the small technical adjustments needed for a photocopied edition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A zine format made from a single A4 sheet folded in half to produce an eight-page A5 booklet. One side of the sheet carries pages 1, 4, 5, and 8; the other carries pages 2, 3, 6, and 7.
Artwork that extends to the cut or fold edge of a page rather than leaving a white margin. In photocopied zines, bleed is achieved by drawing right to the edge of the sheet before folding.

content.text: format places the cover in the top-right quarter of one side of the A4 sheet. This is the area that will be visible when the zine is folded and the top of the sheet is at the right. Designing the cover requires thinking about this position relative to the full sheet before folding.

items.name: A4 paper, 80 gsm
items.notes: Dummy sheets for layout planning before drawing the final version.

items.name: Pencil (for layout)
items.notes: Draw the full cover in pencil first, including all text and image areas. Erase and adjust before committing with ink.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm
items.notes: For ruling margin lines and baseline grids.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For a clean central fold when assembling the dummy.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat (optional)
items.notes: Only needed if trimming the final zine after printing.

content.text: Fold an A4 dummy sheet in half with the bone folder. Open it flat. The four rectangles on each side of the sheet correspond to the eight pages. Label them in sequence: fold the sheet so the cover is visible and mark that rectangle as the cover. Open the sheet and mark all eight page positions. This template stays under the drawing paper as a position guide.
The title should be the largest text element on the cover and readable from a short distance. Allow a margin of at least 8 mm on all sides, this area may be lost to photocopy alignment variation. For

artwork, extend the image right to the fold and cut edges; it will survive as bleed even if the photocopy is slightly off-centre. Avoid fine detail in the corners, which are most vulnerable to misalignment.

Hand lettering on A5 should be drawn larger than the intended print size then reduced on photocopying if needed. Lettering that looks right at A4 scale often looks cramped and heavy at A5. Draw the title at A4 size first, photocopy it reduced to A5, and assess before committing to the final. Thin strokes in hand lettering disappear under photocopying, use strokes no thinner than 1 mm at final size.
